one_line_takeaway: BDefects4NN is a 1,654-model, neuron-labeled backdoor-defect benchmark showing that today's fault-localization criteria find injected backdoor neurons poorly (17.64% mean WJI, weight-based best, activation-based near-useless), and that this weakness carries over to lane-detection and LLM settings.
key_finding: Existing fault-localization methods are largely ineffective at pinpointing backdoor neurons: averaged over the six criteria the mean WJI is only 17.64% on CIFAR-10. Weight-based criteria dominate activation-based ones by roughly 9.4x (CLP 41.80% and ANP 36.05% mean WJI, vs NC 5.97% and FP 2.31%), with general methods in between (SLICER 12.48%, deepmufl 7.23%). Effectiveness degrades as the infected sub-network grows (CLP drops from 81.83% WJI at narrow level to 18.80% at large) and is ~2.21x higher on VGG than on ResNet (skip-connections hide infected neurons). Repair tracks localization quality: pruning gives 39.54% ASRD / 15.29% CAD and fine-tuning 41.45% ASRD / 10.47% CAD on average, far below the PFL oracle. In the case studies localization essentially fails: FP reaches only 4.10% WJI on infected LaneATT and 0.26% WJI on infected ChatGLM, and pruning FP-identified neurons leaves the LLM backdoor almost intact (ASR still 99.63%).
core_contribution: BDefects4NN, the first backdoor-defect database that provides neuron-granularity, ground-truth-labeled backdoor-infected DNNs to enable controlled fault-localization studies. Unlike prior DNN fault databases that only cover unintentional functional bugs (e.g., wrong tensor shapes) and unlike TrojAI/BackdoorBench which inject/evaluate whole-model backdoors without labeling WHICH neurons are faulty, BDefects4NN injects backdoors into deliberately-selected sub-networks so the exact infected neuron set (Sfault) is known. The paper's own contributions are: (1) a defect-injection protocol built on three rules (neuron contribution ordering via NeuronMCT, four neuron-quantity levels, and a sub-network-correlation filter requiring ASR.Cor>0.5); (2) a database of 1,654 infected DNNs across 4 attacks x 4 architectures x 3 datasets, organized into 48 directories; (3) an empirical study evaluating six fault-localization criteria and two repair techniques against this ground truth using a Weighted Jaccard Index (WJI) metric; and (4) two case studies extending backdoor-defect localization to lane detection and an LLM.
threat_model: DNN supply-chain / third-party model reuse. A resource-limited developer downloads or outsources training of a pre-trained third-party DNN and integrates it as middleware into an intelligent software system. The untrusted model may contain intentionally-injected backdoor defects (from poison-training or sub-network replacement) that behave normally on benign inputs but are activated by an attacker-specified trigger to force a target output (e.g., an infected lane-detection model steering off-road when it sees two traffic cones). The developer cannot afford to retrain and instead needs fault-localization tools to identify and repair the specific faulty neurons. The paper studies whether existing localization/repair methods can actually find these neurons; the attacker capability (backdoor injection) is assumed and used to BUILD the labeled benchmark, not attacked as a novel exploit.
limitations: (1) The database is overwhelmingly image-classification CNNs; the ASR.Cor>0.5 correlation filter means only a subset of candidate sub-networks are retained (621/70.57% on CIFAR-10, 456/51.82% on CIFAR-100, 577/65.57% on GTSRB), so 'infected models' are biased toward highly-separable backdoor sub-networks and CIFAR-100 is under-represented due to lower neuron redundancy. (2) The ground-truth Sfault is defined by the authors' injection choice, not a property intrinsic to an arbitrary in-the-wild backdoor; masking remaining neurons also drops ASR (e.g., 70.96% ASR.Cor.R on GTSRB for poisoning attacks), so 'the injected sub-network is the root cause' rests on a modeling assumption. (3) The LLM and lane-detection extensions are single-model case studies where only FP could be applied (other criteria are not portable to transformers / class-free detectors), so conclusions there rest on one weak baseline. (4) Authors acknowledge internal-validity threats from re-implementing localization/repair methods and external-validity threats from the finite set of attacks/architectures/datasets. (5) The paper diagnoses that current methods fail but does not propose a new localization technique.

quantitative_results: Database size: 1,654 infected DNNs across 48 directories; retained yields 621 (70.57%) on CIFAR-10, 456 (51.82%) on CIFAR-100, 577 (65.57%) on GTSRB
55 candidate sub-networks per DNN (20 narrow / 20 small / 10 middle / 5 large)
Infected models: avg 99.28% ASR with only 7.23% CA sacrifice vs benign; injected sub-networks avg 88.79% ASR.Cor.I vs 10.07% CA.Cor.I
Mean localization WJI on CIFAR-10: CLP 41.80%, ANP 36.05%, SLICER 12.48%, deepmufl 7.23%, NC 5.97%, FP 2.31% (six-criteria average 17.64%)
CLP WJI drops from 81.83% (narrow) to 18.80% (large); localization ~2.21x better on VGG than ResNet
Localization time (mean seconds, CIFAR-10): CLP 6, FP 10, ANP 389, NC 716, SLICER 2,474, deepmufl 22,830
Repair pruning: 39.54% ASRD / 15.29% CAD average; CLP pruning up to 94.90% ASRD; FP lowest at 4.06% ASRD
Repair fine-tuning: 41.45% ASRD / 10.47% CAD average; CLP 90.32% and ANP 85.55% ASRD, FP 1.67% / NC 3.88%
Discussion extensions: 94 infected models (Invisible/DFST/SIG on VGG-13/CIFAR-10), 47 (MobileNetV2 + WideResNet-16-4), 25 (Imagenette/ResNet-18)
LaneATT case study: 0.6 deg clean vs 24.8 deg poisoned rotation angle; FP 4.10% WJI; pruning -8.51 deg, fine-tuning -7.49 deg backdoor reduction
ChatGLM-6B case study: infected model 95.29% CA / 99.70% ASR; FP 0.26% WJI; after pruning FP neurons ASR still 99.63% (CA 95.41%)
baselines_compared: Localization criteria: FP, NC (activation-based); ANP, CLP (weight-based); deepmufl (mutation-based); SLICER (least-contribution); PFL oracle upper bound
Backdoor attacks used to inject defects: BadNets, Blended, TrojanNN, SRA (main); Invisible, DFST, SIG (discussion)
Repair methods: neuron pruning, neuron fine-tuning
Related databases contrasted (not run as baselines): TrojAI, BackdoorBench, DeepLocalize/DeepDiagnosis/deepmufl functional-fault databases
